speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. This is roll number 3.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when you got to Lyon?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lyon, the-- the organization-- excuse me. The organization didn't know what to do with us, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the whole time, the organization had kept up with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [COUGHS] Sorry. [DRINKS] The organization-- this organization, also with the French underground, they worked together. And since we couldn't go to Switzerland, they had to find some places for us to hide in. They found a small village not far from Lyon, between Lyon and Saint-Etienne. And, like I said, everybody needed a lot of help, because the men were in prison in Germany. And they found a place for three of us in a village. We went and worked in a farm. The farmer didn't know, naturally, we were only French citizen but come from Alsace, which is now German, because the German took over Elsass-Lothringen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, we don't know what happened to them. And they asked me what religion I was. I said I was Catholic. And one friend of mine was smarter. He said he's Protestant. So at least he didn't have to go to church, but we had to go to church every Sunday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because this was a Catholic family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Very-- very religious. Well, it was very hard for us to work in a farm, because we are not used to working in the wine country and things like that. And he was a tough man. He-- he took a lot of advantage of us. We worked for peanuts, so to speak, for food, to eat and sleep. But at least we were alive. We had to get up at 5:00 in the morning, wash the cows, clean the cows, milk the cows. All the hard work, we worked. And one day, the gendarme-- a policeman-- came and checked our papers, and he sees that I came from Mutzig. I never knew what Mutzig is. The only thing is Mutzig was very well-known for beer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the policeman see I come from Mutzig, oh, he said, I was in Mutzig during the First World War. I was in the hotel there and there, near the bistro, and we were drinking. It's still there? I said, yes. I had no father, but I know I had to make believe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, the situation got worse. They start to-- oh, yeah, this I have to say. You know, the situation got worse and worse. The Germans were front of Stalingrad and Moscow. They ran over all Europe. But my hope was always they're never going to conquer the world or all Europe. One time, the German had a beautiful picture of all of Europe and showed you where the German army are, in front of Moscow and all those places. And I had it in my attic where I lived, and I always watch it. But at night, I made myself a little radio with some crystal. At that time, we had a little crystal, and you could-- with an earphone you could hear. And I used to listen to the BBC. And I had now two different things what happened. And I found out, little by little, the German getting beating. And for me, it was a lot of-- a lot of hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could understand English by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they spoke French.

speaker: interviewer
text: They spoke French on the BBC.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The French-- you know, they tell you in French, in German, or any kind of language at that time. So they-- I found out that it's getting bad. Then I found out that the American debarked in North Africa and then, naturally, in Normandy. The French underground started to get very active there. First, I wanted to join the French underground. But there was such a-- how would I say-- they fought each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: The French underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: The socialist against the-- the communist, this group against the other group. Didn't have no ammunition. They had to steal from this one, and they raided some villages. It was not right. In early 1944, the American liberated Lyon. The German blew up all the bridges except one in Lyon. But soon they-- Lyon was liberated, I ran away from the farmer. I didn't say, well, I go, what else, because he treated us very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the three of you stay together, your friends that you were there with?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. One friend of mine went to a college. He had some connection and went to a college. And I understand he joined, after, the French underground, too. My other friend and myself-- and I wanted to join the American army. I went to a captain, and I told him who I was. I showed him my false papers, but he didn't believe me. He said, you know, there is so many Germans, soldier, who wanted to-- want to join the American army. And he said, if you are what you are, you'd better try to get in touch as soon as possible with your organization. And he marked down in my passport-- French paper, there-- that I'm not-- that it's no good, not valid. So I had to rush to find some people, Jewish people, who can vouch for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had lost connection with the committee by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the committee, we lost completely, because the invasion. And a lot of them got caught and were deported. We never heard of them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever hear from your parents again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have to come back to that. I was in touch with my parents through another organization. I sent a letter [INAUDIBLE], and they sent a letter there. I found out that my parents, in November 1942, were arrested and sent to Drancy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Except my father was sick, and he was taken to the Mount Sinai-- no, Rothschild Hospital in Paris. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] From-- from Drancy, I didn't hear nothing no more for my parents or sister. I found out my father's in-- in the hospital, and I got in touch with him somehow, again, through some connection-- underground connection. And in 19-- late '43 or beginning '44, he was taken and sent to Drancy. And from there, nothing no more, either. They kept him, being sick, in the hospital for so-and-so many months. And yes, so I went back to the organization. I found them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You found them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, give me my papers, and I would like to join the American army. They asked me, please don't do it. Stay with us, because we have hundreds and thousands of children hidden all over, in convents, in French farmers, and so on. And we need somebody-- we need people who can get them out of there again. That's what I did. I stayed with the organization, and we looked for children. And some children didn't want to come back to us. Some children were changed to be Catholic and didn't want to be Jewish again. It was a very difficult situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where exactly did you go? I mean, do you remember any of the homes you went into?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- the organization knew some places where they'd hidden--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name-- this was still the same, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Same organization, OSE.

speaker: interviewer
text: OSE-- oh, the same.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oeuvre de Secours aux Enfants. They started to come back again who were underground, and they formed again the whole thing. And then they got a lot of help from the American Joint, money, advice, and so on. Right after then, the Joint was very powerful, and they helped us tremendously. Also, the Baron of Rothschild left us a lot of money for those children's' homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave you food and clothes, and you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Clothes and--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a job, so to speak of.

speaker: interviewer
text: A real job.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those people who were still alive, those who were in Spain or Russian and Polish and so on, came out from the underground, and we formed again the-- the organization. Like I said, it was very difficult to find the children and to get them back. Now we had to find where are the parents of those children. Most of them were gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there documentation for that? I mean, could you just not find them, or did you know that--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were documentation, and some of them where it got lost. That was the difficult part. We asked people who had hidden children to come forward and tell us. But a lot of the children, we never know. And they kept the children there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were mostly-- you were in France at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In France, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the French people come forward with this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them. Some. I don't know the others, which we never knew. So with those children, we opened up again new children homes, and we put them in there. And then we start to try to find out what happened to the family and the parents and so on. After the war, Drancy was a distribution center. We found all the papers from every single person who was deported from France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those Germans had-- exactly. There were about over 70,000, or almost 80,000 people deported from France. I know exactly when my parents and sister were deported, which transport, the number-- the name of the transport, and so on. And it says only 'direction Auschwitz.' That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So-- and that was after the war. And then the [INAUDIBLE], [? Klarsfeld, ?] she made a book, and she found out how many people came back from this transport. And she has a book of that, which I have, too, here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the feeling in France at that time, among your-- I mean, the war was still going on. Because this was before May of '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you're talking about the-- I'm talking about, now, that after the war-- I mean, the war is still on, but not in France. They--

speaker: interviewer
text: But not in France.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. That's what I'm talking. Now, the French people [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were not that nice. You know, they could do much more for-- they didn't have to pick us up. The Germans said, look, we need 5,000 Jews. Go and get them. Instead of 5,000 Jews, they picked up 10,000 and gave it to Germans. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a lot of cooperation with the Germans, too. And I'm not saying that all French-- some Frenchmen were very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was it like in France after the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, right after the liberation, everybody was breathing freedom. Those people who were collaborating, naturally they got caught and got punished certain ways, and so on. But all of a sudden, those people who were collaborating, they say, oh, I'm going to join the French underground. I am now a Frenchman again, you know. Wishy-washy, like this. For us, our job was to try to find family, find children, and do a new life for ourself. I just want to give you a little example. The French government at that time accepted 500 boys from Buchenwald. That was after the war. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- since I spoke French and German and Yiddish a little, I was chosen to be in camp with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had a special camp for them. And this group, they were German, Polish, Czechoslovak, Hungarian, Russian boys from about eight to about 20-- 20 years old. In that group was Elie Wiesel. I remember him. In this camp, already, we were together. And-- What happened in this camp? What kind of facilities did they have? What were the problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: The facility was just to-- to teach them how to speak French and to get them a new life together. We had-- all of a sudden, we had a lot of food. And some of them, they didn't know how to eat. They ate so much that they got sick. They were blown up, and some of them passed away. They died because of the things. They were wild.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were wild?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were wild. Because they-- freedom for them was something else. They did a couple of revolution in that camp, too. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was all boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: All boys. Then little by little, they were separated. Some of them went to Palestine. Some of them went to France, had some relatives there. And some were-- they got in touch with the United States or England. They had some relatives there, and they get them. The rest of them, we incorporated with the other children home. The older one who were pretty well-- need some education, they sent them to Paris, and some of them went to the Sorbonne and did very well. Like Elie Weisel, you know, I don't have to tell you, did very well. There was a little boy who was about 10, 12 years old who went to Israel, and he is now the big rabbi of Israel, Rabbi Lau. And I'm still in touch with those, because we kept in touch all these years. We have-- we get together every four years sort of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: This group of boys that came out of Buchenwald, you're talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, for myself, my uncle found out that I am still alive, and he sent me an affidavit to come to the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother got also liberated. And he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he when--

speaker: interviewee
text: At different places. Southern France. He got caught one time by the French, and they didn't believe that he was-- had false papers, and they beat the hell out of him. And, sure enough, he admitted. But they let him go.

speaker: interviewer
text: They let him go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was lucky. And after the war, he was also in a children home, but we-- a different children home. Anyway, I got the visa to come to the United States with my brother. And I left in December 1946 to come to the United States. I came here December 29, 1946, arrived in the United States. I remember the Statue of Liberty. And my friends and relatives waited for me in the quay. It was my dream. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I had a cousin in Baltimore and an uncle in Hartford, and each one wanted us to stay with them. So we decide we're going to stay a week there and then stay a week there, in Baltimore, and then we went in Hartford. So we decided to stay in Hartford. I got myself a job right away in a bake shop as a helper, cleaning pots and pans and whatever. I didn't speak English. And that's the way I-- I kept on going to work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a friend of mine, who was a butcher-- he was in a concentration camp and came to the States-- we went to night school. And he wanted to go to Florida. When I heard Florida, Miami, I said, when are you going? Take me with you. He had a car.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in-- it must have been, '48, '49-- we left Hartford, Connecticut, and we drove down to Miami. Well, when I saw Miami Beach again, I said, I promise myself to come back here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back a little bit to the time between your liberation in Lyon, which was '44, and the end of the war in all of Europe, which was '45. And during that time, you worked for the organization to gather the children together?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit more about how that worked? I mean, you didn't go knocking on doors?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but we had-- people who hid those children knew. But a lot of people who were-- this organization-- were deported, and they didn't know. A lot of documents were destroyed or was taken, and so we didn't know. But we had some ideas that in this convent, there was some children there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went there. Some of the nuns don't want to tell you. Some of the children we got out, they were so brainwashed that they told us, even after, that they're going to go to Israel and convert the Jews to-- Mhm. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Catholicism, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Catholicism, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you see, it was very difficult. And then also, the children naturally were without parents. They were hidden without the parents. But then they want their parents back, and they were not there. It was a very difficult-- difficult situation for the children. And we had wonderful people who understood us, who helped us. We didn't have psychiatrists or things like-- there was only people, down-to-earth, who--

speaker: interviewer
text: But all of you were children. I mean, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you were still a children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at this time, I was already 18-- 18 years old, you know? I was already a grown-up.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had been on your own since you were  13, for all intents and purposes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, but you are-- at this time, you grow up very fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You learn, and you have to learn. So we were grown up at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come, you think, you survived? I mean, you-- beside luck. I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: Believe me, I-- human nature has a lot of strength. And I feel that I survived by luck. Pure-- pure luck. Because if I would be a week older, I would be gone. I might have been caught in the train, or my--

speaker: interviewer
text: The farmer was there. He could say something that is not kosher, or something like this. He sees that I get some letters there what's in German language, and I hid them, and so on. He-- he know that I was not quite-- things like that, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I feel it's just plain luck. It-- it was a shock, naturally, to learn that my parents and sister were gone. And I knew what happened. My uncle-- I forgot to tell you about my uncle. He was hidden in a small village, about five homes, all the way down in southern France, for almost two years. He never went out during the daytime, only at night. An old lady hid him in the-- in the attic there, and she fed him. And he gave them some money, whatever, and he survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He left-- he stayed in France. He just passed away last year, after so many years. But I feel it's just plain luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: You feel like it was just plain luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because I feel, what else? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know, we were very religious people in Germany. We were kosher. Sure, we had the store open on Saturday, Friday, but we went to shul every Friday night. On Saturday, all the holidays, we went to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even in a small-- in a small synagogue as a child, I was the shammes there, in the synagogue. I did my bar mitzvah. But I always say, what happened to the six million? But you were saved by a Jewish organization that really looked after you and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: --many of the other children all throughout the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. That's right. And all those people are dedicated people. And I think they were more dedicated to us than to themselves, because to take such a risk what they did was very dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything special you would like to say to your children or your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my children--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your granddaughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's not even two years old. But my-- first of all, may I come back to when I came to the States, here?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. Please do.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in Hartford, and I came here to Miami, and I started to work here in the wintertime. In the summertime, I was in the Catskill Mountains. I worked there for six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then in 1950, I got a letter from the draft-- draftees, and Uncle Sam said, I need you. I want you. In 1939, when I was outside here, Miami Beach, he didn't want me. But the Korean War was on, and he drafted me in the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: I passed my interpreter test as German and French, but I was sent to Korea. Well, I was lucky there again, because I was mostly stationed in Japan. And after, I worked as a baker, too. Even I made some good cake for General Mark Clark, and he asked me if I would like to be his pastry chef there. But I have to be more many years in the army [INAUDIBLE]. But I said no. I wanted to be free. And I came back to the-- out of the army, and I came back to Florida. I started to work-- there was a job open as a helper at the Fontainebleau Hotel when they opened up in 1954, and I took that job. I worked there for 12 years. In the meantime, I was always in touch with this organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a wonderful woman who's from Russia, Mme. Azure, she was like a mother to us. And I went to visit her one time, and she said, would you like to be a pen pal with a girl from France? She would like to be with somebody. I said, certainly. So I got to be a pen pal with a girl for three years. And I said, gee, I would like to meet this girl one time. So I decided to go to France. But I said to myself, I've been to France. It would be a mitzvah to bring her over here. So I sent her a ticket, and she came here to the United States. And that was Vera. That's my wife. That's the way we met, after-- she was also in a children home from there. She was in a children's home in-- in France?

speaker: interviewee
text: In France, yeah. But she was born in France, in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she was born a lot later than You were

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was born in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I think we can probably stop now and do some of the pictures.
